card.intuition: Compare the heads of two sorted lists, attach the smaller one to the result, and recurse — like merging two sorted decks of cards by always picking the smaller top card.
card.analogy: Two checkout lines at a grocery store, both already ordered by arrival time. A manager redirects the next customer from whichever line has the earlier arrival — one combined line forms automatically.
card.space_complexity: O(1) iterative with a dummy head; O(n + m) recursive due to call stack
when_to_use: Merging two sorted linked lists (bottom half of merge sort on lists)
Merging k sorted lists — use a min-heap of size k, popping the smallest head each time
Interleaving two streams that are each individually sorted
gotchas: Use a dummy head node to avoid special-casing the first node — tail starts at dummy, result is dummy.next
After the loop one list will be non-empty; attach it in a single step (tail.next = l1 or l2), not another loop
For k-way merge with a heap, push (node.val, index, node) to break ties — pure node comparison crashes in Python 3

exercise.prompt: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list. Use a dummy head to simplify the logic.
hints: After attaching a node to the result, advance that list's pointer to its next node
The dummy head trick means you never need to check if the result list is empty — just start appending to dummy
exercise.test_code:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val; self.next = next
def make(vs):
    d=ListNode(); c=d
    for v in vs: c.next=ListNode(v); c=c.next
    return d.next
def arr(h):
    r=[]
    while h: r.append(h.val); h=h.next
    return r
assert arr(merge_two_lists(make([1,2,4]),make([1,3,4]))) == [1,1,2,3,4,4]
assert arr(merge_two_lists(make([]),make([])))            == []
assert arr(merge_two_lists(make([]),make([0])))           == [0]
assert arr(merge_two_lists(make([1,3,5]),make([2,4,6]))) == [1,2,3,4,5,6]
print('PASS')
